content:
# Acts Chapter 19 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 248-250 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_019_page_001.jpg, ACT_019_page_002.jpg, ACT_019_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass, while Apollos was in Corinth, that Paul, having passed through the upper regions, came to Ephesus and found certain disciples.

**2** And he said to them, "Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you believed?" And they said to him, "We have not even heard whether there is a Holy Spirit."

**3** And he said, "Into what then were you baptized?" And they said, "Into the baptism of John."

**4** And Paul said, "John baptized with a baptism of repentance, telling the people that they should believe in the one coming after him, that is, in Jesus."

**5** And having heard this, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.

**6** And when Paul laid his hands upon them, the Holy Spirit came upon them, and they were speaking in tongues and prophesying.

**7** And they were in all about twelve men.

**8** And having entered the synagogue, he was speaking boldly for three months, reasoning and persuading concerning the kingdom of God.

**9** But when some were hardened and were disobedient, speaking evil of the Way before the multitude, he withdrew from them and separated the disciples, reasoning daily in the lecture hall of Tyrannus.

**10** And this continued for two years, so that all who dwelt in Asia heard the word of the Lord, both Jews and Greeks.

**11** And God was performing extraordinary miracles through the hands of Paul,

**12** so that even handkerchiefs or aprons were carried from his skin to the sick, and the diseases departed from them, and the evil spirits went out.

**13** And certain of the itinerant Jewish exorcists attempted to name the name of the Lord Jesus over those who had evil spirits, saying, "I adjure you by the Jesus whom Paul proclaims."

**14** And there were seven sons of a certain Sceva, a Jewish chief priest, doing this.

**15** But the evil spirit answering said to them, "Jesus I know, and Paul I recognize, but who are you?"

**16** And the man in whom the evil spirit was leaped upon them and, having overpowered both of them, prevailed against them, so that they fled out of that house naked and wounded.

**17** And this became known to all, both Jews and Greeks, who dwelt in Ephesus, and fear fell upon them all, and the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified.

**18** And many of those who had believed came confessing and declaring their deeds.

**19** And a considerable number of those who had practiced the magic arts brought together their books and burned them before all. And they counted up the value of them and found it to be fifty thousand pieces of silver.

**20** So according to the might of the Lord, the word was growing and prevailing.

**21** And when these things were fulfilled, Paul purposed in the Spirit to pass through Macedonia and Achaia and go to Jerusalem, saying, "After I have been there, I must also see Rome."

**22** And having sent into Macedonia two of those who ministered to him, Timothy and Erastus, he himself stayed in Asia for a time.

**23** And about that time there arose no small disturbance concerning the Way.

**24** For a certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith, who made silver shrines of Artemis, brought no small business to the craftsmen,

**25** whom he gathered together, along with the workers in similar trades, and said, "Men, you know that from this business comes our prosperity.

**26** And you see and hear that not only in Ephesus but in nearly all of Asia, this Paul has persuaded and turned away a considerable crowd, saying that they are not gods, those made by hands.

**27** And not only is there danger that this trade of ours will come into disrepute, but also that the temple of the great goddess Artemis may be reckoned as nothing, and that she whom all Asia and the inhabited world worships should be deprived of her majesty."

**28** And having heard this and becoming full of wrath, they were crying out, saying, "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!"

**29** And the city was filled with confusion, and they rushed with one accord into the theater, having seized Gaius and Aristarchus, Macedonians, traveling companions of Paul.

**30** And when Paul wished to go in to the assembly, the disciples would not allow him.

**31** And some also of the Asiarchs, being friends of his, sent to him and urged him not to venture into the theater.

**32** Some therefore were crying out one thing and some another, for the assembly was in confusion, and the majority did not know for what reason they had come together.

**33** And from the crowd they put forward Alexander, the Jews pushing him forward. And Alexander, motioning with his hand, wished to make a defense before the assembly.

**34** But when they recognized that he was a Jew, one voice arose from all, crying out for about two hours, "Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!"

**35** And when the town clerk had quieted the crowd, he said, "Men of Ephesus, what man is there who does not know that the city of the Ephesians is temple guardian of the great Artemis and of the image that fell from heaven?

**36** Since therefore these things are undeniable, it is necessary for you to be calm and to do nothing rash.

**37** For you have brought these men who are neither temple robbers nor blasphemers of our goddess.

**38** If then Demetrius and the craftsmen with him have a complaint against anyone, court days are held and there are proconsuls; let them bring charges against one another.

**39** But if you seek anything concerning other matters, it shall be settled in the lawful assembly.

**40** For indeed we are in danger of being charged with insurrection concerning today, there being no cause for which we shall be able to give an account of this disorderly gathering."

**41** And having said these things, he dismissed the assembly.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 19 narrates Paul's extended ministry in Ephesus, the most significant single-city engagement in Acts. The chapter divides into four movements: the encounter with John's disciples and the coming of the Spirit (vv. 1-7), Paul's two-year teaching ministry and the extraordinary miracles (vv. 8-20), and the Demetrius riot that closes the Ephesian period (vv. 23-41), with vv. 21-22 serving as a hinge pointing toward Jerusalem and Rome.

**Manuscript condition:** The text of Acts 19 begins on page 248 (image 001), in the rightmost column where Acts 19:2 is marked at the bottom. The text continues through page 249 (image 002), spanning Acts 19:10 through 19:29, and concludes on page 250 (image 003), where Acts 19:36 is marked and the chapter ends before Acts 20:3. The scriptio continua is clear throughout with standard nomina sacra visible: IS/IHN (Jesus), KS/KU/KW (Lord), QS/QU (God), PNA/PNMA (Spirit). The text is well preserved in the primary scribal hand.

**Key textual issue -- verse 16:** The most significant manuscript question in this chapter is the Sinaiticus reading AMPHOTERWN ("both") in v. 16, despite seven sons being named in v. 14. This dual form suggests that only two of the seven were present at this particular encounter. SOPHIA preserves "both" as the faithful rendering of Sinaiticus against LOGOS and PNEUMA, who read "all." The dual is the lectio difficilior; scribes would naturally change "both" to "all" to harmonize with "seven sons," not the reverse.

**Key textual issue -- verse 9:** Sinaiticus omits the Western text's addition "from the fifth hour to the tenth" (found in Codex Bezae), which specifies that Paul taught during the midday hours when the hall was vacant. All three translators correctly follow the shorter Sinaiticus reading.

**Key textual issue -- verse 15:** The evil spirit's response uses two distinct Greek verbs for "know": ginosko (experiential knowledge) for Jesus and epistamai (cognitive recognition) for Paul. EMET renders both as "know/know-about"; LOGOS distinguishes "know/recognize"; PNEUMA gives "know/am well acquainted with." SOPHIA follows LOGOS's distinction as the most accurate and readable rendering of the two verbs.

**Agreement among translators:** The three translations show substantial agreement on the overall narrative. The primary areas of divergence involve: (1) the dual vs. plural in v. 16, (2) the rendering of the two "know" verbs in v. 15, (3) the source-text reading "skin" vs. "body" in v. 12, (4) the register of the town clerk's speech in vv. 35-40, (5) word choice for magical terminology in vv. 13 and 19, and (6) various structural differences between EMET's literalism and PNEUMA's dynamic restructuring throughout.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Opening formula | "And it came-to-pass while Apollos was being in Corinth" | "And it came to pass, while Apollos was in Corinth, that" | "While Apollos was in Corinth, Paul traveled through" | "And it came to pass, while Apollos was in Corinth, that" -- follows LOGOS; preserves egeneto de formula; PNEUMA drops it entirely |
| 1 | "was being in" vs. "was in" vs. "was in" | "was being" (einai, periphrastic) | "was in" | "was in" | "was in" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA; English does not require periphrastic |
| 1 | "having-passed-through the upper regions came to Ephesus" vs. "having passed through the upper regions, came to Ephesus" vs. "traveled through the highland regions and came down to Ephesus" | "having-passed-through the upper regions" | "having passed through the upper regions" | "traveled through the highland regions and came down to" | "having passed through the upper regions, came to Ephesus" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "came down" (not in Greek) and renders anoterika as "highland" rather than "upper" |
| 1 | "found certain disciples" vs. "found certain disciples" vs. "found a group of disciples" | "found certain disciples" (tinas mathetas) | "found certain disciples" | "found a group of disciples" | "found certain disciples" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; tinas means "certain/some," not "a group of" |
| 2 | "Did you receive [the] Holy Spirit having-believed?" vs. "Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you believed?" vs. "Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you came to faith?" | "having-believed" (pisteusantes, aorist participle) | "when you believed" | "when you came to faith" | "when you believed" -- follows LOGOS; renders the aorist participle temporally; PNEUMA's "came to faith" is an interpretive expansion of pisteusantes |
| 2 | "But not even whether [the] Holy Spirit is have we heard" vs. "We have not even heard whether there is a Holy Spirit" vs. "We have not even heard that there is a Holy Spirit" | Greek word order preserved; "whether" (ei) | English word order; "whether" | English word order; "that" instead of "whether" | "We have not even heard whether there is a Holy Spirit" -- follows LOGOS; the Greek ei introduces an indirect question ("whether"), not a declarative ("that"); PNEUMA's "that" changes the grammar |
| 3 | "Into what then were you baptized?" identical across all three | "Into what then were you baptized?" | "Into what then were you baptized?" | "Then what baptism did you receive?" | "Into what then were you baptized?" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA restructures eis ti oun ebaptisthete, changing "into what were you baptized" to "what baptism did you receive" |
| 3 | "Into the baptism of John" vs. same vs. "The baptism of John" | "Into the baptism of John" | "Into the baptism of John" | "The baptism of John" | "Into the baptism of John" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA drops eis ("into") |
| 4 | "saying to the people that into the [one] coming after him they should believe" vs. "telling the people that they should believe in the one coming after him" vs. "He told the people to put their trust in the one coming after him" | Greek word order; "saying"; "into" | English word order; "telling"; "in" | "told"; "put their trust in" | "telling the people that they should believe in the one coming after him" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "put their trust in" is an interpretive expansion of pisteusosin eis |
| 4 | "that is, into Jesus" vs. "that is, in Jesus" vs. "that is, in Jesus" | "into Jesus" (eis ton Iesoun) | "in Jesus" | "in Jesus" | "that is, in Jesus" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA; "in" is natural English for the object of belief |
| 5 | "into the name" vs. "in the name" vs. "in the name" | "into the name" (eis to onoma) | "in the name" | "in the name" | "in the name" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA; English idiom for baptism uses "in" |
| 5 | "having-heard [this]" vs. "having heard this" vs. "When they heard this" | Participial | Participial | Temporal clause | "having heard this" -- follows LOGOS; preserves the participial akousantes |
| 6 | "Paul having-laid [his] hands upon them" vs. "when Paul laid his hands upon them" vs. "when Paul placed his hands on them" | Participial genitive absolute | Temporal clause | Temporal clause | "when Paul laid his hands upon them" -- follows LOGOS; renders genitive absolute as temporal clause |
| 6 | "came upon them" -- all three agree | "came upon them" | "came upon them" | "came on them" | "came upon them" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; "upon" matches the Greek epi |
| 6 | "were speaking in tongues and prophesying" vs. same vs. "began speaking in tongues and prophesying" | "were speaking" (imperfect elaloun) | "were speaking" | "began speaking" (inchoative) | "were speaking in tongues and prophesying" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; the imperfect elaloun means "were speaking," not "began speaking"; PNEUMA adds inchoative aspect not in the Greek |
| 7 | "they were, all [the] men, about twelve" vs. "they were in all about twelve men" vs. "There were about twelve men in all" | Greek word order preserved | Rearranged for English | Restructured as existential "There were" | "they were in all about twelve men" -- follows LOGOS |
| 8 | "having-entered into the synagogue" vs. "having entered the synagogue" vs. "Paul went into the synagogue" | "into" explicit; participial | participial | Finite verb; supplies "Paul" as subject | "having entered the synagogue" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA supplies "Paul" (not in Greek) and converts participle to finite verb |
| 8 | "speaking-boldly" vs. "speaking boldly" vs. "spoke boldly" | Hyphenated compound; imperfect | Imperfect | Simple past | "was speaking boldly" -- follows LOGOS; the imperfect eparressiazeto conveys continuous action |
| 8 | "reasoning and persuading [them] concerning" vs. "reasoning and persuading concerning" vs. "engaging people in discussion and persuading them about" | "persuading [them]" | "persuading" (no object) | "engaging people in discussion and persuading them about" | "reasoning and persuading concerning the kingdom of God" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "engaging people in discussion" is an interpretive expansion of dialegomenos |
| 9 | "were being-hardened and were being-disobedient" vs. "were hardened and were disobedient" vs. "became stubborn and refused to believe" | Imperfect passive continuous | Simple past | Dynamic rendering: "stubborn/refused to believe" | "were hardened and were disobedient" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "refused to believe" interprets epeithoun (were disobedient) as specifically about belief, but the word means disobedience broadly |
| 9 | "speaking-evil-of the Way before the multitude" vs. "speaking evil of the Way before the multitude" vs. "publicly slandering the Way in front of the whole congregation" | "the multitude" (tou plethous) | "the multitude" | "the whole congregation" | "the Way before the multitude" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA's "whole congregation" adds "whole" not in Greek and interprets plethos as a congregation |
| 9 | "having-withdrawn from them he separated the disciples" vs. "he withdrew from them and separated the disciples" vs. "Paul broke away from them. He took the disciples with him" | Participial + finite verb | Two finite verbs | Two sentences; "broke away"; "took the disciples with him" | "he withdrew from them and separated the disciples" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "broke away" and "took the disciples with him" are interpretive restructurings |
| 9 | "reasoning daily in the lecture-hall of Tyrannus" vs. "reasoning daily in the lecture hall of Tyrannus" vs. "held daily discussions in the lecture hall of Tyrannus" | "reasoning" (dialegomenos) | "reasoning" | "held daily discussions" | "reasoning daily in the lecture hall of Tyrannus" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA's "held daily discussions" converts the participle to a periphrastic construction |
| 10 | "this happened for two years" vs. "this continued for two years" vs. "This went on for two years" | "happened" (egeneto) | "continued" | "went on" | "this continued for two years" -- follows LOGOS; egeneto ("came to pass/happened") is rendered idiomatically; PNEUMA's "went on" is colloquial |
| 10 | "all those dwelling in Asia" vs. "all who dwelt in Asia" vs. "everyone living in the province of Asia" | "those dwelling" (tous katoikountas) | "who dwelt" | "everyone living in the province of" | "all who dwelt in Asia" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA supplies "the province of" (not in Greek) |
| 11 | "powers not-the-ordinary God was doing through the hands of Paul" vs. "God was performing extraordinary miracles through the hands of Paul" vs. "God was doing extraordinary miracles through Paul's hands" | "powers not-the-ordinary" (dunameis ou tas tuchousas); Greek word order | "extraordinary miracles" | "extraordinary miracles" | "God was performing extraordinary miracles through the hands of Paul" -- follows LOGOS; the litotes ou tas tuchousas ("not the ordinary kind") is rendered as "extraordinary"; EMET preserves Greek word order |
| 12 | "from his skin" vs. "from his body" vs. "that had touched his skin" | "from his skin" (apo tou chromatos) | "from his body" | "that had touched his skin" | "from his skin" -- follows EMET/PNEUMA (2-to-1); Sinaiticus reads chromatos ("skin/surface"), not somatos ("body"); LOGOS renders "body" which does not match the Sinaiticus reading |
| 12 | "handkerchiefs or aprons" vs. "handkerchiefs or aprons" vs. "handkerchiefs and work-aprons" | "handkerchiefs or aprons" (sudaria e simikinthia) | "handkerchiefs or aprons" | "handkerchiefs and work-aprons" | "handkerchiefs or aprons" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; Greek uses e ("or"), not "and"; PNEUMA changes conjunction and adds "work-" |
| 12 | "upon the sick were being-carried-away" vs. "were carried...to the sick" vs. "were carried to the sick" | "upon the sick were being-carried-away" (Greek word order) | "were carried...to the sick" | "were carried to the sick" | "were carried...to the sick" -- follows LOGOS; natural English word order |
| 12 | "the diseases were being-released from them" vs. "the diseases departed from them" vs. "their diseases left them" | "were being-released" (apallassesthai) | "departed" | "left" | "the diseases departed from them" -- follows LOGOS; apallasso means "to release, depart" |
| 12 | "the evil spirits were going-out" vs. "the evil spirits went out" vs. "evil spirits came out" | "were going-out" (imperfect) | "went out" | "came out" | "the evil spirits went out" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "came out" changes the directional perspective |
| 13 | "some of the wandering Jews, exorcists" vs. "certain also of the itinerant Jewish exorcists" vs. "Some Jewish exorcists who traveled from place to place" | "wandering Jews, exorcists" | "itinerant Jewish exorcists" | "Jewish exorcists who traveled from place to place" | "certain of the itinerant Jewish exorcists" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA expands perierchomai ("itinerant/wandering") into a relative clause |
| 13 | "attempted to name over those having the evil spirits the name of the Lord Jesus" vs. "attempted to name the name of the Lord Jesus over those who had evil spirits" vs. "tried invoking the name of the Lord Jesus over people who had evil spirits" | Greek word order | English word order | "invoking" instead of "name"; "people" instead of "those" | "attempted to name the name of the Lord Jesus over those who had evil spirits" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "invoking" replaces the literal onomatizein ("to name") |
| 13 | "I adjure you by the Jesus whom Paul proclaims" vs. same vs. "I command you by the Jesus that Paul preaches" | "I adjure" (horkizo) | "I adjure" | "I command" | "I adjure you by the Jesus whom Paul proclaims" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; horkizo means "to adjure/put under oath," not "to command"; PNEUMA's "command" loses the oath-formula register |
| 13 | "proclaims" vs. "proclaims" vs. "preaches" | "proclaims" (keryssei) | "proclaims" | "preaches" | "proclaims" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; kerysso means "to proclaim/herald" |
| 14 | "there were certain [men], sons of Sceva" vs. "there were seven sons of a certain Sceva" vs. "Seven sons of a Jewish chief priest named Sceva" | "certain [men], sons of Sceva, a Jewish chief-priest, seven [sons]" | "seven sons of a certain Sceva, a Jewish chief priest" | "Seven sons of a Jewish chief priest named Sceva" | "there were seven sons of a certain Sceva, a Jewish chief priest" -- follows LOGOS; EMET preserves the awkward Greek word order; PNEUMA drops "there were" and restructures |
| **15** | **Two Greek verbs for "know" -- ginosko vs. epistamai** | "Jesus I know (ginosko), and Paul I know-about (epistamai)" | "Jesus I know (ginosko), and Paul I recognize (epistamai)" | "Jesus I know (ginosko), and Paul I am well acquainted with (epistamai)" | **"Jesus I know, and Paul I recognize, but who are you?"** -- follows LOGOS; ginosko and epistamai are distinct verbs; ginosko = experiential knowledge, epistamai = cognitive recognition/acquaintance; EMET's "know-about" is adequate but less precise; PNEUMA's "am well acquainted with" is an expansion; LOGOS's "know/recognize" best captures the distinction concisely |
| 15 | "the evil spirit answering said" vs. "the evil spirit answering said" vs. "the evil spirit answered them" | "answering said" (apokrithenti...eipen) | "answering said" | "answered them" | "the evil spirit answering said to them" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; preserves the Hebraic "answering said" formula |
| **16** | **"both" (amphoterown) vs. "all" -- major textual variant** | "having-overpowered both [of them]" (amphoteron) | "overpowered them all" | "overpowered them all" | **"having overpowered both of them, prevailed against them"** -- follows EMET (1-to-2 override); Sinaiticus reads AMPHOTERWN ("both"), the dual form, despite seven sons being named in v. 14; this is the lectio difficilior and implies only two of the seven were present at this encounter; LOGOS/PNEUMA harmonize to "all" which smooths the difficulty but departs from the manuscript; see Disagreement Log |
| 16 | "having-leaped upon them" vs. "leaped upon them" vs. "leaped on them" | Participial | Finite verb | Finite verb; "on" vs. "upon" | "leaped upon them" -- follows LOGOS |
| 16 | "prevailed against them" vs. (omitted -- merged into "overpowered") vs. (omitted -- merged into "overpowered") | "prevailed against them" (ischysen kat' auton) | Not rendered separately | "beat them so savagely" | "prevailed against them" -- follows EMET; the Greek has two distinct actions: katakyrieusas (having overpowered) and ischysen (prevailed); LOGOS collapses them; PNEUMA replaces with "beat them so savagely" (interpretive expansion) |
| 16 | "naked and having-been-wounded" vs. "naked and wounded" vs. "naked and bleeding" | "having-been-wounded" (tetraumatismenous) | "wounded" | "bleeding" | "naked and wounded" -- follows LOGOS; tetraumatizo means "to wound"; PNEUMA's "bleeding" specifies a type of wound not in the Greek |
| 16 | "fled out of that house" -- all agree on substance | "fled out of that house" | "fled out of that house" | "fled from that house" | "fled out of that house" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; preserves ek ("out of") |
| 17 | "those dwelling in Ephesus" vs. "who dwelt in Ephesus" vs. "everyone living in Ephesus" | "those dwelling" | "who dwelt" | "everyone living" | "who dwelt in Ephesus" -- follows LOGOS |
| 17 | "the name of the Lord Jesus was being-magnified" vs. "was magnified" vs. "was held in the highest honor" | "was being-magnified" (imperfect emegalyneto) | "was magnified" | "was held in the highest honor" | "was magnified" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "held in the highest honor" is an interpretive expansion of megalyno ("to magnify/make great") |
| 18 | "confessing and declaring their deeds" vs. "confessing and declaring their practices" vs. "openly confessing and exposing their former practices" | "their deeds" (tas praxeis auton) | "their practices" | "openly...exposing their former practices" | "confessing and declaring their deeds" -- follows EMET; praxis means "deed/action"; LOGOS's "practices" and PNEUMA's "former practices" add connotation of habitual activity; PNEUMA also adds "openly" and "exposing" and "former" (none in Greek) |
| 18 | "many of those having-believed were coming" vs. "many of those who had believed came" vs. "Many who had come to believe now came forward" | "were coming" (imperfect erchonto) | "came" (simple past) | "came forward" (adds "forward"); "had come to believe" (expands) | "many of those who had believed came" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "came forward" and "had come to believe" are expansions |
| 19 | "those having-practiced the magic-arts" vs. "those who practiced magic" vs. "those who had practiced sorcery" | "magic-arts" (ta perierga) | "magic" | "sorcery" | "the magic arts" -- follows EMET; ta perierga literally means "the curious/superfluous arts" (a euphemism for magic); "sorcery" (PNEUMA) is more pejorative than the Greek; "magic" (LOGOS) is adequate but less specific |
| 19 | "having-brought-together the books, were burning [them]" vs. "brought together their books and burned them" vs. "brought their scrolls together and burned them" | "the books" (tas biblous) | "their books" | "their scrolls" | "brought together their books and burned them" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "scrolls" is a specific interpretation of biblous (which means "books/written documents" broadly); both "books" and "scrolls" are defensible but "books" is the standard rendering |
| 19 | "counted-up the prices of them" vs. "counted the value of them" vs. "added up the value" | "the prices" (tas timas) | "the value" | "the value" | "counted up the value of them" -- composite; timas can mean "prices" or "value"; "value" is more natural in English |
| 19 | "fifty thousand [pieces] of silver" vs. "fifty thousand pieces of silver" vs. "fifty thousand pieces of silver" | Brackets around "pieces" (supplied) | "pieces" | "pieces" | "fifty thousand pieces of silver" -- unanimous on substance; EMET's brackets note the supplied word |
| **20** | **"according to the might of the Lord" vs. "mightily" vs. "In this powerful way"** | "according-to [the] might of the Lord the word was growing and was prevailing" (kata kratos tou kyriou) | "the word of the Lord was growing mightily and prevailing" | "In this powerful way, the word of the Lord kept spreading and growing in strength" | **"So according to the might of the Lord, the word was growing and prevailing"** -- follows EMET (1-to-2 override); Sinaiticus reads KATA KRATOS TOU KURIOU, which means "according to the might of the Lord" (tou kyriou modifies kratos, not logou); LOGOS reattaches "of the Lord" to "the word" and renders kata kratos adverbially as "mightily"; EMET correctly parses the Greek phrase; PNEUMA paraphrases entirely |
| 20 | "was growing and was prevailing" vs. "was growing...and prevailing" vs. "kept spreading and growing in strength" | "growing and prevailing" (euxanen kai ischyen) | "growing...and prevailing" | "kept spreading and growing in strength" | "was growing and prevailing" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA's "kept spreading" replaces auxano ("to grow") and "growing in strength" replaces ischyo ("to prevail/be strong") -- restructured |
| 21 | "when these things were fulfilled" vs. "when these things were fulfilled" vs. "After all this had happened" | "were fulfilled" (eplerothe) | "were fulfilled" | "had happened" | "when these things were fulfilled" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; pleroo means "to fulfill/complete," not merely "to happen"; PNEUMA's "had happened" loses the sense of divine purpose |
| 21 | "purposed in the Spirit" vs. "purposed in the Spirit" vs. "resolved in the Spirit" | "purposed" (etheto) | "purposed" | "resolved" | "purposed in the Spirit" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; etheto en to pneumati means "set/placed in the Spirit"; "purposed" is the traditional rendering |
| 21 | "having-passed-through Macedonia and Achaia, to go to Jerusalem" vs. "to pass through Macedonia and Achaia and go to Jerusalem" vs. "to travel through Macedonia and Achaia and then go on to Jerusalem" | Participial construction | Infinitive construction | "travel through...and then go on to" | "to pass through Macedonia and Achaia and go to Jerusalem" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "then" and "on to" |
| 21 | "it is necessary [for] me also to see Rome" vs. "I must also see Rome" vs. "I must also see Rome" | "it is necessary [for] me" (dei me) | "I must" | "I must" | "I must also see Rome" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA; dei me is rendered naturally as "I must" |
| 22 | "two of those ministering to him" vs. "two of those who ministered to him" vs. "two of his assistants" | "those ministering" (ton diakonounton) | "those who ministered" | "his assistants" | "two of those who ministered to him" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "assistants" interprets diakonounton, which means "those serving/ministering" |
| 22 | "he himself held-on for a time in Asia" vs. "he himself stayed in Asia for a time" vs. "he himself stayed on in the province of Asia for a time" | "held-on" (epeschen) | "stayed" | "stayed on in the province of" | "he himself stayed in Asia for a time" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA again supplies "the province of" |
| 23 | "there arose at that time no small disturbance" vs. "about that time there arose no small disturbance" vs. "a major disturbance erupted over the Way" | "at that time no small disturbance" | "about that time...no small disturbance" | "a major disturbance erupted" | "about that time there arose no small disturbance" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA replaces the litotes "no small" (ouk oligos) with "major" and uses "erupted" for egeneto |
| 24 | "a certain-one, Demetrius by name, a silversmith" vs. "a certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith" vs. "A silversmith named Demetrius" | "a certain-one, Demetrius by name" (tis onomati Demetrios) | "a certain man named Demetrius" | "A silversmith named Demetrius" | "a certain man named Demetrius, a silversmith" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA drops tis ("certain") and reorders |
| 24 | "making silver shrines of Artemis" vs. "who made silver shrines of Artemis" vs. "who made silver shrines of Artemis" | Participial "making" | Relative clause "who made" | Relative clause "who made" | "who made silver shrines of Artemis" -- follows LOGOS/PNEUMA |
| 24 | "was providing no small business to the craftsmen" vs. "brought no small business to the craftsmen" vs. "brought in considerable business for the craftsmen" | "was providing no small business" | "brought no small business" | "brought in considerable business" | "brought no small business to the craftsmen" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA again replaces litotes "no small" with "considerable" |
| 25 | "whom having-gathered-together, and the workers concerning such things" vs. "whom he gathered together, along with the workers in similar trades" vs. "gathered them together along with workers in related trades" | "the workers concerning such things" (tous peri ta toiauta ergatas) | "workers in similar trades" | "workers in related trades" | "along with the workers in similar trades" -- follows LOGOS; "similar" and "related" are both acceptable for toiauta |
| 25 | "Men, you know that from this business is our prosperity" vs. "Men, you know that from this business comes our prosperity" vs. "Men, you know very well that our prosperity comes from this trade" | "from this business is our prosperity" (Greek word order) | "from this business comes our prosperity" | "our prosperity comes from this trade" (reversed; adds "very well") | "Men, you know that from this business comes our prosperity" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "very well" (not in Greek) and reverses the clause order |
| 26 | "this Paul having-persuaded [them] turned-away a considerable crowd" vs. "this Paul has persuaded and turned away a considerable multitude" vs. "this Paul has persuaded and turned away large numbers of people" | "a considerable crowd" (hikanon ochlon) | "a considerable multitude" | "large numbers of people" | "a considerable crowd" -- follows EMET; ochlos means "crowd"; LOGOS's "multitude" is archaic; PNEUMA's "large numbers of people" loses the singular collective noun |
| 26 | "saying that they are not gods, those being-made by hands" vs. "saying that gods made with hands are not gods" vs. "by telling them that gods made by human hands are not gods at all" | Greek word order; "by hands" (dia cheiron) | English word order; "with hands" | "by human hands...not gods at all" | "saying that they are not gods, those made by hands" -- follows EMET; preserves the emphatic Greek word order where the denial comes first; PNEUMA adds "human" and "at all" (not in Greek) |
| 27 | "this [is] in-danger for us, the part to come into disrepute" vs. "there danger that this trade of ours will come into disrepute" vs. "There is a danger...that our trade will fall into disrepute" | "the part" (to meros) | "this trade" | "our trade" | "this trade of ours will come into disrepute" -- follows LOGOS; to meros means "the part/portion" but refers to their trade; LOGOS supplies the referent naturally |
| 27 | "the temple of the great goddess Artemis to be reckoned as nothing" vs. "the temple...may be counted as nothing" vs. "the temple...will be regarded as worthless" | "reckoned as nothing" (eis outhen logisthentai) | "counted as nothing" | "regarded as worthless" | "may be reckoned as nothing" -- composite; PNEUMA's "worthless" interprets outhen ("nothing") as "worthless" which is close but not exact |
| 27 | "to be about to be pulled-down [from] her majesty" vs. "should be deprived of her majesty" vs. "will be stripped of her majesty" | "pulled-down" (kathairein) | "deprived" | "stripped" | "should be deprived of her majesty" -- follows LOGOS; kathaireo means "to pull down, demolish, depose"; "deprived" captures the loss of status; PNEUMA's "stripped" is more violent than the Greek; EMET's "pulled-down" is very literal |
| 27 | "whom all Asia and the inhabited-world worships" vs. "she whom all Asia and the inhabited world worships" vs. "she herself -- whom all of Asia and the entire world worship" | "whom" | "she whom" | "she herself -- whom" (adds dash and emphasis); "entire world" | "she whom all Asia and the inhabited world worships" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "she herself" adds emphasis and "entire" is not in Greek |
| 28 | "having-become full of anger" vs. "becoming full of wrath" vs. "were filled with rage" | "anger" (thumou) | "wrath" | "rage" | "becoming full of wrath" -- follows LOGOS; thumos can be "anger," "wrath," or "rage"; "wrath" is a middle register appropriate for narrative |
| 28 | "were crying-out saying" vs. "were crying out, saying" vs. "began shouting" | "crying-out saying" (ekrazon legontes) | "crying out, saying" | "began shouting" | "were crying out, saying" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA drops legontes ("saying") and adds inchoative "began" |
| 29 | "the city was filled with the confusion" vs. "the city was filled with confusion" vs. "The whole city was thrown into chaos" | "the confusion" (tes sygchyseos, with article) | "confusion" (drops article) | "chaos" (dynamic); adds "whole"; "thrown into" | "the city was filled with confusion" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "whole" and uses "chaos" for sygchysis and restructures the passive |
| 29 | "having-seized Gaius and Aristarchus" vs. "having seized Gaius and Aristarchus" vs. "The crowd seized Gaius and Aristarchus" | Participial | Participial | "The crowd seized" -- adds subject "The crowd"; finite verb | "having seized Gaius and Aristarchus" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA supplies "the crowd" as subject |
| 29 | "traveling-companions of Paul" vs. "traveling companions of Paul" vs. "Paul's Macedonian traveling companions" | "traveling-companions of Paul" | "traveling companions of Paul" | "Paul's Macedonian traveling companions" | "traveling companions of Paul" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA moves "Macedonian" into the descriptor |
| 30 | "Paul wanting to enter into the populace" vs. "when Paul wished to go in to the assembly" vs. "Paul wanted to go before the crowd" | "the populace" (ton demon) | "the assembly" | "the crowd" | "when Paul wished to go in to the assembly" -- follows LOGOS; demos means "the people/assembly" (civic sense); PNEUMA's "crowd" loses the civic connotation |
| 30 | "the disciples were not allowing him" vs. "the disciples would not allow him" vs. "the disciples would not let him" | "were not allowing" (ouk eion) | "would not allow" | "would not let" | "the disciples would not allow him" -- follows LOGOS; "let" (PNEUMA) is adequate but less formal |
| 31 | "some also of the Asiarchs" vs. "some also of the Asiarchs" vs. "some of the provincial officials" | "Asiarchs" (Asiarchon) | "Asiarchs" | "provincial officials" | "some also of the Asiarchs" -- follows EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA's "provincial officials" interprets the specific title Asiarchai, which were prestigious civic/religious officers of the province of Asia; the specific term should be preserved |
| 31 | "being friends to him" vs. "being friends of his" vs. "who were friendly to him" | "friends to him" | "friends of his" | "who were friendly to him" | "being friends of his" -- follows LOGOS |
| 31 | "having-sent to him, were urging [him] not to give himself into the theater" vs. "sent to him and urged him not to venture into the theater" vs. "sent an urgent message begging him not to set foot in the theater" | "urging him not to give himself" (dounai heauton) | "urged him not to venture" | "sent an urgent message begging him not to set foot" | "sent to him and urged him not to venture into the theater" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "urgent message begging" and "set foot" are dramatic expansions |
| 32 | "Others then were crying-out some other-thing" vs. "Some therefore were crying out one thing and some another" vs. "Some were shouting one thing, some another" | "Others then" (alloi oun) | "Some therefore" | "Some" (drops connector) | "Some therefore were crying out one thing and some another" -- follows LOGOS |
| 32 | "the assembly was having-been-confused" vs. "the assembly was in confusion" vs. "the assembly was in total confusion" | "was having-been-confused" (pluperfect synkechymene) | "was in confusion" | "was in total confusion" (adds "total") | "was in confusion" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "total" is not in the Greek |
| 32 | "the majority did not know for what reason they had come-together" vs. same vs. "Most of the people had no idea why they had even gathered" | "the majority" (hoi pleious) | "the majority" | "Most of the people" | "the majority did not know for what reason they had come together" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "had no idea" and "even gathered" are colloquial expansions |
| 33 | "they put-forward Alexander, the Jews having-pushed him forward" vs. "they put forward Alexander, the Jews pushing him forward" vs. "Some in the crowd put forward Alexander, and the Jews pushed him to the front" | "having-pushed him forward" (probebasanton, aorist) | "pushing him forward" (present) | "pushed him to the front" (adds "to the front") | "the Jews pushing him forward" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "to the front" is an addition |
| 33 | "having-motioned [with] the hand" vs. "motioning with his hand" vs. "motioned with his hand" | Participial aorist | Participial present | Finite verb | "motioning with his hand" -- follows LOGOS |
| 33 | "was wanting to make-a-defense to the populace" vs. "wished to make a defense before the assembly" vs. "trying to make a defense speech to the people" | "to the populace" (to demo) | "before the assembly" | "to the people" | "wished to make a defense before the assembly" -- follows LOGOS; consistent with v. 30 rendering of demos |
| 34 | "having-recognized that he is a Jew" vs. "when they recognized that he was a Jew" vs. "the moment they recognized he was a Jew" | "having-recognized" (epignontes) | "when they recognized" | "the moment they recognized" | "when they recognized that he was a Jew" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "the moment" adds immediacy not in the participle |
| 34 | "one voice came from all" vs. "one voice arose from all" vs. "a single shout went up from them all" | "one voice came" (phone egeneto mia) | "one voice arose" | "a single shout went up" | "one voice arose from all" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "single shout" and "went up" are interpretive |
| 34 | "crying-out for about two hours" vs. "crying out for about two hours" vs. "they kept chanting" | "crying-out" (krazontes) | "crying out" | "kept chanting" | "crying out for about two hours" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "chanting" interprets the action as rhythmic repetition, which is plausible but goes beyond krazo ("to cry out") |
| **35** | **Town clerk's title and speech register** | "the town-clerk having-quieted the crowd says" (historic present) | "when the town clerk had quieted the crowd, he said" | "Finally the city clerk quieted the crowd and said" | "when the town clerk had quieted the crowd, he said" -- follows LOGOS; EMET preserves historic present (legei/"says"); PNEUMA adds "Finally" (not in Greek) and uses "city clerk" instead of "town clerk" for grammateus |
| 35 | "Men, Ephesians" vs. "Men of Ephesus" vs. "People of Ephesus" | "Men, Ephesians" (andres Ephesioi) | "Men of Ephesus" | "People of Ephesus" | "Men of Ephesus" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's gender-neutral "People" departs from andres (specifically "men"); EMET's "Men, Ephesians" preserves the vocative apposition |
| 35 | "who is [there] of men who does not know" vs. "what man is there who does not know" vs. "Who in all the world does not know" | "who is of men" (tis...anthropon) | "what man is there" | "Who in all the world" (adds "in all the world") | "what man is there who does not know" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "in all the world" is a rhetorical expansion |
| 35 | "being temple-keeper of the great Artemis" vs. "is temple guardian of the great Artemis" vs. "is the guardian of the temple of the great Artemis" | "temple-keeper" (neakoron) | "temple guardian" | "guardian of the temple" | "is temple guardian of the great Artemis" -- follows LOGOS; neokoros is a single honorific title ("temple warden"); PNEUMA splits it into "guardian of the temple" |
| 35 | "of the [image] fallen-from-Zeus" vs. "of the image that fell from heaven" vs. "of the sacred stone that fell from heaven" | "fallen-from-Zeus" (tou diopetous) | "image that fell from heaven" | "sacred stone that fell from heaven" | "of the image that fell from heaven" -- follows LOGOS; diopetes literally means "Zeus-fallen" but idiomatically "fallen from heaven"; PNEUMA's "sacred stone" interprets the object as a meteorite, which is one scholarly view but not in the Greek text |
| 36 | "these-things being undeniable" vs. "since therefore these things are undeniable" vs. "Since these facts are beyond dispute" | "being undeniable" (anantirrheton) | "are undeniable" | "are beyond dispute" | "since therefore these things are undeniable" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "facts" replaces "things" and "beyond dispute" paraphrases anantirrhetos |
| 36 | "it is necessary [for] you to be having-been-quieted" vs. "it is necessary for you to be calm" vs. "you need to calm down" | "to be having-been-quieted" (katestalmenous) | "to be calm" | "to calm down" | "it is necessary for you to be calm" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "calm down" is colloquial; the perfect participle katestalmenous means "to be in a settled/calm state" |
| 36 | "to do nothing reckless" vs. "to do nothing rash" vs. "not do anything reckless" | "reckless" (propetes) | "rash" | "reckless" | "to do nothing rash" -- follows LOGOS; both "reckless" and "rash" are acceptable for propetes |
| 37 | "you brought these men, neither temple-robbers nor blaspheming our goddess" vs. "you have brought these men who are neither temple robbers nor blasphemers of our goddess" vs. "You have brought these men here, but they have neither robbed temples nor blasphemed our goddess" | "temple-robbers nor blaspheming" (hierosylous oute blasphemountas) | "temple robbers nor blasphemers" | "neither robbed temples nor blasphemed" (converts to verbs) | "you have brought these men who are neither temple robbers nor blasphemers of our goddess" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA converts the nominal forms to verbs and adds "here" and "but" |
| 38 | "have a matter against anyone" vs. "have a complaint against anyone" vs. "have a legal complaint against anyone" | "a matter" (logon) | "a complaint" | "a legal complaint" | "have a complaint against anyone" -- follows LOGOS; logon here means "case/matter/complaint"; PNEUMA adds "legal" (not explicit in Greek) |
| 38 | "court-days are being-held and there are proconsuls" vs. "court days are held, and there are proconsuls" vs. "the courts are in session and there are proconsuls" | "court-days are being-held" (agoraioi agontai) | "court days are held" | "the courts are in session" | "court days are held and there are proconsuls" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "courts are in session" modernizes the specific term agoraioi ("court days/assizes") |
| 38 | "let them bring-charges against one-another" vs. "let them bring charges against one another" vs. "let them bring charges against each other there" | "one-another" | "one another" | "each other there" (adds "there") | "let them bring charges against one another" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "there" |
| 39 | "in the lawful assembly it shall be resolved" vs. "it shall be settled in the lawful assembly" vs. "it must be settled in a lawful assembly" | "resolved" (epilythesetai) | "settled" | "settled"; "a lawful" (indefinite article) | "it shall be settled in the lawful assembly" -- follows LOGOS; both "resolved" and "settled" work for epilyo; PNEUMA's "a lawful" changes the definite article te ennomo to indefinite |
| **40** | **"insurrection" vs. "rioting" vs. "rioting"** | "being-accused of insurrection" (staseos) | "being charged with rioting" | "being charged with rioting" | "being charged with insurrection" -- composite; stasis means "standing, sedition, insurrection" -- a more politically serious term than "rioting"; "insurrection" (EMET) preserves the gravity of the Roman legal charge; "charged" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is better than "accused" for the legal context |
| 40 | "concerning today" vs. "concerning this day's events" vs. "over today's events" | "concerning today" (peri tes semeron) | "concerning this day's events" | "over today's events" | "concerning today" -- follows EMET; the Greek says simply peri tes semeron ("concerning today"), without "events" |
| 40 | "there being no cause concerning which" vs. "there being no cause for which" vs. "since there is no legitimate reason we can give" | "no cause" (meden aition) | "no cause" | "no legitimate reason" (adds "legitimate") | "there being no cause for which we shall be able to give an account" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "legitimate" and restructures |
| 40 | "of this disorderly-gathering" vs. "of this disorderly gathering" vs. "to justify this mob" | "disorderly-gathering" (systrophes) | "disorderly gathering" | "this mob" | "of this disorderly gathering" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "mob" is a dynamic rendering of systrophe ("a gathering together, commotion") and "justify" replaces "give an account of" |
| 41 | "having-said these-things, he dismissed the assembly" vs. "having said these things, he dismissed the assembly" vs. "with these words, he dismissed the assembly" | "having-said these-things" | "having said these things" | "with these words" | "having said these things, he dismissed the assembly" -- follows LOGOS; PNEUMA's "with these words" is a natural English rendering but departs from the participial tauta eipon |

-- SOPHIA
